title: On Gadamer's Prejudice Against Prejudice
author: Manish Maheshwari
body:
Gadamer's rehabilitation of Vorurteil is not a defence of unreflective bias. It is the much more radical claim that the Enlightenment's demand for prejudice-free reason is itself a prejudice — the deepest and most consequential of them. To understand at all is always to understand from somewhere.

The mistake is to read this as an endorsement of whatever one happens to believe. It is rather an invitation to take one's own tradition seriously enough to interrogate it — which cannot be done from nowhere.

title: Dubois on Temple Dancers
author: Manish Maheshwari
body: In the third chapter of the Description, Dubois turns his attention to the devadāsī — and here his prose shifts from anthropological description to something closer to moral vertigo. The tension between fascination and condemnation structures every sentence. One senses a man who has seen something he cannot place in his categories, and whose instinct is to resolve the placement problem by moral disapproval rather than by expanding the categories.

title: A Fragment on Pratyabhijñā
author: Manish Maheshwari
body:
Recognition, in Utpaladeva's sense, is not the discovery of something new. It is the remembering of what was always already the case. The student who recognises the teaching has not acquired information; she has returned to a position she never, in truth, left.

This makes Kashmir Śaivism strangely hospitable to the person who feels they have no starting point. There is nowhere to start from, in fact — because there was never any departure.

title: On Reading Inscriptions in the Rain
author: Manish Maheshwari

body.text: The stone at Talakāḍ is nearly unreadable in direct sun. In the rain, though, water pools in the cuts and the letters emerge — a provisional legibility, conferred by weather.

The whole practice of reading stones is built on such accidents. The epigraphist does not so much decipher as wait — for the right angle of light, for humidity, for the slow erosion that sometimes reveals what earlier erosion had concealed.

title: Why Translation is not Betrayal
author: Manish Maheshwari
body:
The Italian joke — traduttore, traditore — has made a small career of flattering the untranslatable. But the premise is wrong. Betrayal implies a settled meaning that could, in principle, be faithfully transmitted. What translation actually reveals is that no such settled meaning exists even in the original. A Sanskrit word like rasa does not mean one thing in the Nāṭyaśāstra that is then lost in English; it means several things in the Nāṭyaśāstra, some of them in tension, which English has to choose between.

This is not betrayal. This is the ordinary weather of all reading.

title: Marginalia from a 19th-Century Gītā
author: Manish Maheshwari
body: A copy of Telang's 1882 translation of the Gītā, found in a secondhand shop in Mysore. The owner — whoever she was — has underlined almost nothing in the first six chapters and almost everything in the eleventh. The theophany evidently mattered to her more than the yoga of action. One learns something about a reader by what they underline, and something else by what they do not.

title: On Not Finishing Books
author: Manish Maheshwari
body: I am slowly becoming reconciled to the fact that I will not finish most of the books I start, and I am no longer sure this is a failing. Some books are meant to be finished; many are meant to be consulted. The culture of reading that requires every book to be completed is a culture that has confused books with novels.

title: A Question for Historians
author: Manish Maheshwari
body: When we say that such-and-such an idea was held in a particular period, what we usually mean is that the idea survives in the written record we happen to have. The silent majority of medieval South Indians — farmers, weavers, blacksmiths, everyone — left almost nothing behind that we can read. We reconstruct their mental world from the fragments of the literate minority who wrote about them, often condescendingly. This is not history. It is the history of a tiny, over-documented class projected onto everyone else.
